oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Knots/knives/knerves
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Griffin, Dee
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: The size of cattle and situations with which we work is inher­ently dangerous. Important safe cattle handling includes the use of HACCP (Hazard Analysis Critical Control Points). The emphasis can be summarized with the acronym SAFE (safety of yourself, safety of the animal, safety of the food and safety of everyone around the animal). Included is a description of mak­ing a pole syringe for delivering sedatives to fractious cattle Restraint frequently requires the use of rope along with knots used with restraint ropes. This is a review of knots I have found useful in safety working with cattle. Knives are frequently used in our daily interaction with cattle. For example, necropsy knives. As the saying goes: the most dangerous knife is a dull knife. This is a primer on knife sharp­ening both manual and motorized. Included are ways to maxi­mize the duration of a sharp knife edge using alternative cut­ting instruments other than a knife. The “knerves” portion is intended by the AABP Recent Graduate Conference developers as a lead into the use of dehorning local anesthetic and not an overview a wide range of local anesthetic techniques. Surgical dehorning causes acute and post-procedure pain. There are specific details for the use of the cornual nerve block along with thoughts on improving efficacy. Included is a brief discussion on meloxicam use to help manage post-surgical pain. Additionally, the paper includes the directions for making and using meloxicam oral tablets in cattle rather than using gela­tin capsules to contain the meloxicam tablets being dosed.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: American Association of Bovine Practitioners Conference Proceedings; No. 1 (2026): 2026 Recent Graduate Conference Proceedings; 75-86
